top_supported.caption: Performance of J48 for different feature settings
top_supported.claim: Specifically, using all features except phonological features is equivalent to using phonological features alone (about F = 79% in both cases) and slightly worse that using all name-intrinsic features (about F = 80%).

top_supported.caption: Quantitative comparison (Rouge 1, 2 and L) of models on aspect-specific summarization.
top_supported.claim: Table 1 (top) presents results of models trained and tested on the synthetic multi-aspect dataset.

top_supported.caption: P@k, R@k, and macro-F1 results over all labels (the union of S, F, and Z).
top_supported.claim: Furthermore, we find that ACNN outperforms ZAGCNN in P@10 by almost 4%.

top_supported.caption: Automatic semantic evaluation (higher is better for all but SER).
top_supported.claim: Table 6 presents the average SER rates for each model, where lower rates mean fewer mistakes (indicated by ?).

top_supported.caption: Quantitative comparison (Rouge 1, 2 and L) of models on aspect-specific summarization.
top_supported.claim: All aspect-aware models beat both baselines by a large margin.

top_supported.caption: Automatic evaluation results for the task of question response generation. Numbers in bold mean that the improvement over the best performing baseline is statistically significant (t-test with p-value< 0.01).
top_supported.claim: We can see that S2S-Temp outperforms all baseline models in terms of all metrics, and the improvements are statistically significant (t-test with p-value< 0.01).

top_supported.caption: The results of case analysis (Case) and zero anaphora resolution (Zero). We use F-measure as an evaluation measure. ‡ denotes that the improvement is statistically significant at p < 0.05, compared with Gen using paired t-test.

Hate speech results (3-fold cross val.). In most cells we show performance without thresholding and stratification (within bracket with thresholding and stratification). Green: Best performance in each column.
top_supported.claim: Table 6 shows hate speech detection results.

top_supported.caption: Hate speech results (3-fold cross val.). In most cells we show performance without thresholding and stratification (within bracket with thresholding and stratification). Green: Best performance in each column.
top_supported.claim: Remarkably, synthetic text alone outperforms gold text, because gold text has high class imbalance, leading to poorer prediction.

top_supported.caption: The results of case analysis (Case) and zero anaphora resolution (Zero). We use F-measure as an evaluation measure. ‡ denotes that the improvement is statistically significant at p < 0.05, compared with Gen using paired t-test.
top_supported.claim: Our models (Gen and Gen+Adv) outperformed the previous models.

top_supported.caption: Scores for MRR and Top k results.
top_supported.claim: Table 3 shows that our AP algorithm outperforms all 3 baseline methods.

top_supported.caption: P@k, R@k, and macro-F1 results over all labels (the union of S, F, and Z).
top_supported.claim: We find that R@10 is nearly equivalent to the R@10 on the frequent group in Table 3.

top_supported.caption: Results (F1) for baselines for sentence boundary detection on dev sets.
top_supported.claim: Table 2 shows the accuracies of all baseline systems on the development sets.

top_supported.caption: Automatic evaluation results for the task of question response generation. Numbers in bold mean that the improvement over the best performing baseline is statistically significant (t-test with p-value< 0.01).
top_supported.claim: Table 1 report the results of automatic evaluation on the question response generation task.

top_supported.caption: Comparison with another chatbot.
top_supported.claim: The Kappa measure between the analysts is 0.71, which shows a substantial agreement.

top_refuted.caption: Automatic semantic evaluation (higher is better for all but SER).
top_refuted.claim: Naturally, BASE, with no access to style information, has the best (lowest) SER.

top_refuted.caption: Average (from six runs) parsing results (LAS) on test sets. † marks statistical significance (p-value < 0.05). Corresponding standard deviations are provided in Table 3 in Appendix A.
top_refuted.claim: It shows that the differences between models are not restricted to arcs of particular lengths.

top_refuted.caption: Average (from six runs) parsing results (LAS) on test sets. † marks statistical significance (p-value < 0.05). Corresponding standard deviations are provided in Table 3 in Appendix A.
top_refuted.claim: Comparing the simple and extended architectures we see that dropping the structural features does not hurt the performance, neither for transition-based nor graph-based parsers.

top_refuted.caption: The results of case analysis (Case) and zero anaphora resolution (Zero). We use F-measure as an evaluation measure. ‡ denotes that the improvement is statistically significant at p < 0.05, compared with Gen using paired t-test.
top_refuted.claim: Furthermore, the proposed model with adversarial training (Gen+Adv) was significantly better than the supervised model (Gen).

top_refuted.caption: P@k, R@k, and macro-F1 results over all labels (the union of S, F, and Z).
top_refuted.claim: Both R@k and P@k give more weight to frequent labels, thus it is expected that ACNN outperforms ZAGCNN for frequent labels.

top_refuted.caption: Automatic semantic evaluation (higher is better for all but SER).
top_refuted.claim: But we note that there is not a large increase in SER as more information is added – even for the most difficult setting, +STYLE, the models make an error on less than 10% of the slots in a given MR, on average.

top_refuted.caption: Scores for MRR and Top k results.
top_refuted.claim: These results show that our AP method produces more correct answers at the top of the list than the baseline methods.

top_nei.caption: DA classification results (%) using different systems.
top_nei.claim: Table 6 summarizes the results for different systems, including the baseline CNN model without using context information (this baseline uses pretrained embeddings and speaker change feature), and four different ways of using context: (a) predicted DA information (posterior probabilities) is combined with the current sentence's CNN-based representation; (b) applying a BLSTM on top of the sentence CNN representation; (c) hierarchical CNN that combines the current sentence's CNN representation with its neighbors; (d) sequence decoding by combining CNN posteriors with DA transition scores.

top_nei.caption: We show performance in terms of recall, computed per-category and averaged across categories, for the baseline, list and scatterplot interfaces. This is shown for the last round of bootstrapping (c.f. Fig. 3a) and extrapolation (c.f. Fig. 3b).
top_nei.claim: In Table 1 we also show recall, computed per-category and averaged over categories, at the last round of bootstrapping and similarly find the scatterplot outperforms the list.

top_nei.caption: Experiment results
top_nei.claim: Compared to the best systems in SemEval challenge, MIN achieves 3.0% and 1.1% absolute gains on D1 and D2 respectively.

top_nei.caption: We show performance in terms of recall, computed per-category and averaged across categories, for the baseline, list and scatterplot interfaces. This is shown for the last round of bootstrapping (c.f. Fig. 3a) and extrapolation (c.f. Fig. 3b).
top_nei.claim: The recall performance in Table 1 also confirms the performance gains of the scatterplot.

top_nei.caption: Comparison with another chatbot.
top_nei.claim: We select 878 out of the 1047 testing questions (used in the A/B test) by removing questions relevant to our chatbot, and use it to test the public one.

top_nei.caption: Experiment results
top_nei.claim: Besides, our MIN outperforms WDEmb, a strong CRF-based system benefiting from several kinds of useful word embeddings, by 2.1% on D1.

top_nei.caption: Scores for MRR and Top k results.
top_nei.claim: We show results for 5 variations of the algorithm: AP uses Algorithm 1, and the others use Algorithm 2 with different Activity Similarity measures: AP+AL (location profile similarity), AP+AO (overlap similarity), AP+AE (embedding similarity), and AP+AL+E (location profiles plus embeddings).

top_nei.caption: Quantitative comparison (Rouge 1, 2 and L) of models on aspect-specific summarization.
top_nei.claim: For classical summarization, the lead-3 baseline remains a challenge to beat even by state-of-the-art systems, and also on multiaspect documents we observe that, unlike our systems, PG-net performs worse than lead-3.

top_nei.caption: Experiment results
top_nei.claim: With memory interactions and consideration of sentimental sentence, our MIN boosts the performance of vanilla bi-directional LSTM (+2.0% and +1.7% respectively).

top_nei.caption: Experiment results
top_nei.claim: It validates the effectiveness of the manually designed memory operations and the proposed memory interaction mechanism.

top_nei.caption: Hate speech results (3-fold cross val.). In most cells we show performance without thresholding and stratification (within bracket with thresholding and stratification). Green: Best performance in each column.
top_nei.claim: Because we can create arbitrary amounts of synthetic text, we can balance the labels to achieve better prediction.

top_nei.caption: Quantitative comparison (Rouge 1, 2 and L) of models on aspect-specific summarization.
top_nei.claim: Overall, our structured models enable both abstractive and extractive aspectaware summarization at a quality clearly exceeding structure-agnostic baselines.

top_nei.caption: F1 performance of DGLSTM-CRF with predicted dependencies against the best performing BiLSTM-CRF. †: LAS is label attachment score which is the metric for dependency evaluation.
top_nei.claim: Such results suggest that it is essential to have high-quality dependency annotations available for the proposed model.

top_nei.caption: Experiment results
top_nei.claim: To further study the impact of each element in MIN, we conduct ablation experiments.

top_nei.caption: Scores for MRR and Top k results.
top_nei.claim: For these experiments, the system gets full credit if any of its top k answers exactly matches the gold standard, or 50% credit if a partial match is among its top k answers.

top_nei.caption: DA classification results (%) using different systems.
top_nei.claim: From the results, we can see the positive effect when context information is used.

top_nei.caption: Scores for MRR and Top k results.
top_nei.claim: The first 3 rows show the results for the baseline systems, and the remaining rows show results for our Activity Profile (AP) semi-supervised learning method.

top_nei.caption: DA classification results (%) using different systems.
top_nei.claim: Comparing representing context information via the DA labels of the previous utterances vs using the hierarchical CNN or RNN model, we see there is not much difference.

top_nei.caption: Quantitative comparison (Rouge 1, 2 and L) of models on aspect-specific summarization.
top_nei.claim: Unsurprisingly, the extractive aspect-aware models outperform their abstractive counterparts in terms of ROUGE, and the decoder attention distributions are more amenable to extraction than encoder attention scores.

top_nei.caption: DA classification results (%) using different systems.
top_nei.claim: All the methods using context yield significant improvement over the baseline (statistically significant based on t-test).
